Statistical Comparison of Treatment Assignment Designs

Formal Hypothesis Testing for Design Selection in Spatial CRTs

Author

Andrew Walther

Published

April 8, 2026

1 Introduction

This report presents formal statistical hypothesis tests to compare the performance of 8 treatment assignment designs evaluated in the IncidenceDesign simulation study for Spatial Cluster Randomized Trials (CRTs). While prior analyses have characterized design performance descriptively using mean MSE, coverage probability, and win-rate rankings, these comparisons lack the statistical rigor needed to declare one design significantly better than another.

The central question is: can we provide statistical evidence that certain designs yield significantly better estimation accuracy (lower MSE for \(\hat{\tau}\)) than others? Answering this question is essential for providing actionable guidance to investigators planning future spatial CRTs.

The simulation evaluated 2560 scenarios spanning 320 unique parameter combinations, with each combination tested under all 8 designs. This paired structure is exploited by the non-parametric tests described below.

2 Methods

2.1 Overview of the Comparison Problem

We seek to determine whether the 8 treatment assignment designs differ significantly in their ability to estimate the treatment effect \(\tau\), as measured by MSE. This is fundamentally a multiple treatment comparison problem: we have \(k = 8\) designs, each observed under the same set of experimental conditions (parameter combinations), and we wish to test whether any design is systematically better or worse.

Because the same data-generating conditions are shared across all designs within each parameter combination, we have a paired / blocked experimental structure. Standard parametric tests (e.g., repeated-measures ANOVA) assume normally distributed residuals, which may not hold for MSE values. We therefore use non-parametric rank-based tests that make no distributional assumptions.

The total number of unique pairwise comparisons among \(k = 8\) designs is: \[\binom{k}{2} = \binom{8}{2} = 28\]

2.2 Blocking Structure

Each scenario is defined by a unique combination of simulation parameters:

Parameter Levels Count
Incidence Mode iid, spatial, poisson (with \(\rho_X \in \{0.20, 0.50\}\)) 5 configs
Neighbor Type rook, queen 2
\(\rho\) (outcome spatial autocorrelation) 0.00, 0.01, 0.20, 0.50 4
\(\gamma\) (spillover magnitude) 0.50, 0.60, 0.70, 0.80 4
Spillover Type control-only, bidirectional 2

This produces \(5 \times 2 \times 4 \times 4 \times 2 = 320\) blocks. Within each block, all 8 designs were evaluated under identical data-generating conditions, creating a natural repeated-measures / paired design. The metric of interest is the Mean Squared Error (MSE) of the MLE estimator \(\hat{\tau}\) for the primary scenario \(\tau = 1.0\). The tau-sweep sensitivity analysis (\(\tau \in \{0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0\}\)) is presented in Section 6.

Each design’s MSE per block is aggregated from 250 simulation iterations (25 treatment assignment resamples \(\times\) 10 outcome resamples).

2.3 Friedman Test (Omnibus)

The Friedman test (Friedman, 1937) is a non-parametric alternative to one-way repeated-measures ANOVA. It is the standard method for comparing multiple treatments or methods measured under matched conditions.

Procedure: Within each of the \(N = 320\) blocks, the \(k = 8\) designs are ranked by MSE (rank 1 = lowest MSE = best performance). The test then evaluates whether the average ranks differ across designs more than expected by chance.

Hypotheses:

  • \(H_0\): All \(k\) designs have equal average MSE ranks across the \(N\) blocks. No design is systematically better or worse.
  • \(H_1\): At least one design’s average rank differs from the others.

The Friedman test statistic is: \[Q = \frac{12N}{k(k+1)} \left[\sum_{j=1}^{k} \bar{R}_j^2 - \frac{k(k+1)^2}{4}\right]\]

where \(\bar{R}_j\) is the average rank of design \(j\) across all blocks. Under \(H_0\), \(Q\) follows a \(\chi^2\) distribution with \(k - 1 = 7\) degrees of freedom.

Reference: Friedman, M. (1937). The use of ranks to avoid the assumption of normality implicit in the analysis of variance. Journal of the American Statistical Association, 32(200), 675-701.

2.4 Nemenyi Post-hoc Test

When the Friedman test rejects \(H_0\) (indicating at least one design differs), we need post-hoc pairwise comparisons to identify which designs differ. The Nemenyi test (Nemenyi, 1963) is the non-parametric analogue of the Tukey HSD test and controls the family-wise error rate across all \(\binom{k}{2} = 28\) simultaneous comparisons.

Two designs \(i\) and \(j\) are declared significantly different at level \(\alpha\) if and only if their average rank difference \(|\bar{R}_i - \bar{R}_j|\) exceeds the Critical Difference (CD):

\[CD = \frac{q_{\alpha, k, \infty}}{\sqrt{2}} \times \sqrt{\frac{k(k+1)}{6N}}\]

where \(q_{\alpha, k, \infty}\) is the critical value of the Studentized range distribution with \(k\) groups and infinite degrees of freedom, \(k = 8\), and \(N = 320\) blocks.

Results are presented as a Critical Difference (CD) diagram (introduced to the machine learning community by Demšar, 2006). In this diagram:

  • Designs are placed on a horizontal number line by their average rank (lower = better MSE).
  • Thick horizontal bars connect groups of designs that are NOT significantly different from each other — these form equivalence groups.
  • If two designs are not connected by a shared bar, their performance difference is statistically significant.
  • A red arrow at the bottom shows the CD scale for reference.

References:

  • Nemenyi, P.B. (1963). Distribution-free multiple comparisons. Ph.D. dissertation, Princeton University.
  • Demšar, J. (2006). Statistical comparisons of classifiers over multiple data sets. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 7, 1-30.

2.5 Pairwise Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Tests

As a sensitivity analysis and to provide a more widely-known test alongside the Friedman/Nemenyi framework, we also perform pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests (Wilcoxon, 1945) on the paired MSE values.

Hypotheses (for each pair of designs \(i, j\)):

  • \(H_0\): The distribution of paired MSE differences \((\text{MSE}_i - \text{MSE}_j)\) is symmetric about zero. That is, designs \(i\) and \(j\) have equivalent MSE performance.
  • \(H_1\): The distribution is not symmetric about zero. One design systematically outperforms the other.

This yields 28 individual tests. To control the family-wise error rate across all tests, p-values are adjusted using the Holm step-down procedure (Holm, 1979), which is uniformly more powerful than Bonferroni correction while still controlling the Type I error rate.

References:

  • Wilcoxon, F. (1945). Individual comparisons by ranking methods. Biometrics Bulletin, 1(6), 80-83.
  • Holm, S. (1979). A simple sequentially rejective multiple test procedure. Scandinavian Journal of Statistics, 6(2), 65-70.

2.6 Significance Notation

Throughout this report, statistical significance is indicated using the following star notation:

Symbol Meaning Interpretation
*** \(p < 0.001\) Very strong evidence against \(H_0\)
** \(p < 0.01\) Strong evidence against \(H_0\)
* \(p < 0.05\) Moderate evidence against \(H_0\)
ns \(p \geq 0.05\) Insufficient evidence to reject \(H_0\)

3 Results: Aggregate Analysis

3.1 Friedman Omnibus Test

Friedman omnibus test result.
Statistic df P-value N Blocks Decision
1091.90 7 <2e-16 320 Reject H0

The Friedman test yields \(\chi^2 = 1091.90\) with \(7\) degrees of freedom (\(p < 2.2 \times 10^{-16}\)). We reject the null hypothesis: there is overwhelming evidence that the 8 designs differ in their MSE performance.

3.2 Average Ranks

Designs ordered by average MSE rank across all scenarios (1 = best).
Design Average Rank Rank Position
Design 8 2.666 1
Design 3 2.709 2
Design 6 3.622 3
Design 2 3.819 4
Design 7 3.909 5
Design 4 5.772 6
Design 5 6.222 7
Design 1 7.281 8

Designs 3 (Saturation Quadrants) and 8 (Incidence-Guided Saturation Quadrants) occupy the top two positions with nearly identical average ranks (2.666 and 2.709), while Design 1 (Checkerboard) ranks last at 7.281.

3.3 Critical Difference Diagram

How to read this diagram: Each design is positioned on the number line by its average rank across all 320 blocks (lower = better). The thick dark bars above the axis connect designs that are statistically equivalent — i.e., their pairwise Nemenyi p-value is \(\geq 0.05\). Designs that do not share a bar are significantly different from each other.

The red double-arrow labeled “CD” at the bottom shows the Critical Difference threshold: if two designs are separated by more than this distance on the rank axis, their difference is statistically significant.

The CD diagram reveals 3 equivalence group(s):

  • Group 1: Design 8, Design 3 (avg ranks 2.67 – 2.71)
  • Group 2: Design 6, Design 2, Design 7 (avg ranks 3.62 – 3.91)
  • Group 3: Design 4, Design 5 (avg ranks 5.77 – 6.22)

The Nemenyi critical difference is CD = 0.587 at \(\alpha = 0.05\). Designs whose average rank difference exceeds 0.587 are declared significantly different.

3.4 Pairwise Wilcoxon Results

Of the 28 pairwise comparisons, 24 are statistically significant (Holm-adjusted \(p < 0.05\)) and 4 are not.

The non-significant pairs (Wilcoxon) identify designs whose MSE performance is statistically indistinguishable:

Design pairs with no significant MSE difference (Wilcoxon).
Design A Design B Wilcoxon p Nemenyi p
Design 2 Design 6 0.4418 0.9721
Design 2 Design 7 0.8893 0.9998
Design 3 Design 8 0.2467 1.0000
Design 4 Design 5 0.1194 0.2804

3.5 MSE Distribution with Significance

The following boxplot shows the full distribution of scenario-level MSE values for each design, ordered by median MSE (best on the left). Brackets above the boxes with significance stars indicate pairwise comparisons from the Wilcoxon signed-rank test (Holm-adjusted). To avoid visual clutter, up to 8 key comparisons are shown, prioritizing those involving the best- and worst-performing designs.

4 Results: Conditional Analysis

To assess whether design rankings are robust across simulation conditions, we repeat the Friedman test within strata defined by individual parameters. If the Friedman test remains significant within every stratum and the best-ranked designs are consistent, the recommendation is robust.

4.1 Summary of Conditional Friedman Tests

Friedman test results within each parameter stratum. 'Best Group' lists designs in the top equivalence group (statistically indistinguishable from the best).
Parameter Stratum Chi-squared P-value N Blocks Best Group Significant
Rho Rho = 0 336.74 < 2.2e-16 80 D3, D2, D8, D6 Yes
Rho Rho = 0.01 272.14 < 2.2e-16 80 D3, D8, D6, D2 Yes
Rho Rho = 0.2 297.57 < 2.2e-16 80 D8, D3, D6 Yes
Rho Rho = 0.5 223.59 < 2.2e-16 80 D8, D3 Yes
Gamma Gamma = 0.5 266.69 < 2.2e-16 80 D3, D8, D6, D2 Yes
Gamma Gamma = 0.6 272.65 < 2.2e-16 80 D8, D3, D6 Yes
Gamma Gamma = 0.7 279.50 < 2.2e-16 80 D8, D3, D6, D2 Yes
Gamma Gamma = 0.8 274.22 < 2.2e-16 80 D3, D8, D6, D2 Yes
Spillover_Type Spillover_Type = both 597.10 < 2.2e-16 160 D7, D6, D8, D3 Yes
Spillover_Type Spillover_Type = control_only 846.04 < 2.2e-16 160 D8, D3 Yes
Neighbor_Type Neighbor_Type = queen 475.39 < 2.2e-16 160 D3, D8, D2 Yes
Neighbor_Type Neighbor_Type = rook 658.35 < 2.2e-16 160 D8, D3, D6 Yes
Incidence_Mode Incidence_Mode = iid 290.56 < 2.2e-16 64 D8, D2, D3 Yes
Incidence_Mode Incidence_Mode = poisson 390.80 < 2.2e-16 128 D8, D3, D6 Yes
Incidence_Mode Incidence_Mode = spatial 488.47 < 2.2e-16 128 D3, D8, D2 Yes
True_Tau True_Tau = 0.8 1052.77 < 2.2e-16 320 D3, D8 Yes
True_Tau True_Tau = 1 1091.90 < 2.2e-16 320 D8, D3 Yes
True_Tau True_Tau = 1.5 964.05 < 2.2e-16 320 D8, D3, D6 Yes
True_Tau True_Tau = 2 909.95 < 2.2e-16 320 D8, D3, D6 Yes
True_Tau True_Tau = 3 743.41 < 2.2e-16 320 D3, D8, D6 Yes

The Friedman test is significant in every stratum (\(p < 2.2 \times 10^{-16}\) in all cases), indicating that design differences are not an artifact of any particular parameter setting.

The following CD diagrams show how design rankings shift (or remain stable) across levels of key simulation parameters.

5 Discussion

5.1 Key Findings

  1. Overwhelming evidence of design differences. The Friedman test (\(\chi^2 = 1091.90\), \(p < 2.2 \times 10^{-16}\)) decisively rejects the null hypothesis that all designs perform equally. The average rank gap between the best (Design 8, rank 2.67) and worst (Design 1, rank 7.28) design is 4.62 on an 8-point scale.

  2. Design 8 and Design 3 are the top performers. These two designs are statistically indistinguishable from each other (Nemenyi \(p > 0.05\)) and form the top equivalence group in the CD diagram. Both employ quadrant-based saturation strategies, with Design 8 using random saturation assignment and Design 3 guiding saturation by observed incidence.

  3. Design 1 (Checkerboard) is significantly worse than all other designs. The pairwise comparison between Design 8 and Design 1 yields \(p < 0.001\) (Wilcoxon, Holm-adjusted). The checkerboard pattern, while intuitive, fails to account for spatial heterogeneity in outcomes.

  4. Rankings are robust across all simulation parameters. The Friedman test is significant within every stratum tested (all \(p < 2.2 \times 10^{-16}\)), and the top designs remain consistent regardless of the level of spatial autocorrelation, spillover magnitude, spillover type, neighbor contiguity, or incidence generation mode.

5.2 Implications for Study Design

For investigators planning a spatial CRT with spatially heterogeneous outcomes, these results provide strong statistical evidence to recommend saturation quadrant designs (Design 8 or Design 3) over alternative strategies. The recommendation is not sensitive to specific assumptions about the data-generating process within the range of parameters explored.

In particular:

  • Avoid the Checkerboard design (Design 1) — it performs significantly worse than all alternatives under MLE estimation.
  • Saturation quadrant designs leverage spatial structure by assigning different treatment saturation levels to different regions, which provides better identification of the treatment effect under spatial dependence.
  • When prior incidence data is available, Design 3 (Incidence-Guided) may offer a slight advantage by directing higher saturation to higher-incidence areas, though the improvement over Design 8 is not statistically significant.

5.3 Limitations

  • Primary \(\tau = 1.0\): Omnibus and pairwise tests condition on the primary effect size. Sensitivity across \(\tau \in \{0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0\}\) is presented in Section 6; rankings remain stable across effect sizes.
  • Oracle spillover: The MLE estimator includes the true spillover covariate, which may not be available in practice.
  • Simulation-specific: Results are specific to the 10×10 grid, SAR data-generating process, and parameter ranges explored.

6 Tau Sensitivity Analysis

The tau-sweep simulation (12,800 scenarios) swept \(\tau \in \{0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0\}\). The primary question is whether the D3/D8 dominance established at \(\tau = 1.0\) is robust to the effect size assumption.

6.1 Conditional Tests by True Tau Level

Conditional Friedman test results stratified by true tau level. All p-values are below 2.2e-16, confirming that design differences persist across all effect sizes.
Stratum Chi_squared P_value N_blocks Best_Group
True_Tau = 0.8 1052.77 0 320 D3, D8
True_Tau = 1 1091.90 0 320 D8, D3
True_Tau = 1.5 964.05 0 320 D8, D3, D6
True_Tau = 2 909.95 0 320 D8, D3, D6
True_Tau = 3 743.41 0 320 D3, D8, D6

6.2 Power Analysis: Probability of Rejecting H0: τ = 0

6.3 Rank Trajectories Across Tau Values

7 Conclusion

Using a Friedman test with Nemenyi post-hoc comparisons and corroborated by pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, we find:

  • 23 of 28 design pairs are significantly different at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level (Nemenyi post-hoc), and 24 of 28 under the Wilcoxon signed-rank test with Holm correction.
  • Design 8 (Saturation Quadrants) and Design 3 (Incidence-Guided Saturation Quadrants) are the recommended designs, forming the top equivalence group with average ranks of 2.67 and 2.71.
  • These findings are robust across all tested levels of spatial autocorrelation, spillover magnitude, spillover type, neighbor contiguity, and incidence generation mode.

This analysis provides the statistical foundation for recommending specific treatment assignment designs for future spatial CRT studies.
